turns.text: No, Mr. Justice Rehnquist, this Court has applied that principle even in the case of express exemptions. The two cases for example that we cited in our brief, Abbott Laboratories and Federal Maritime Commission versus Seatrains were case of express exemptions from the antitrust laws.

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Do you think that one applies the principle of narrow construction equally to express exemptions as to imply the exemption?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Yes, I do Your Honor. When you're dealing with an express exemption such as the McCarran-Ferguson Act or the many other express exemptions, the principle applies that exemption is to be narrowly construed. In any event, what the legislative history the McCarran Act does indicate is this Court has held in SEC versus Variable Annuity Company and SEC versus National Securities is first that Congress' core concern with respect to the business of insurance was the underwriting risk and the relationships between insurance companies and policyholders that concern the underwriting of risk. And the second thing the legislative history indicates is that Congress did not intend to exempt everything an insurance company does. Those two facts support our contention that the pharmacy agreements are not the business of insurance. First, the pharmacy agreements are not the underwriting of risk. They are simply contracts for the purchase of drugs and drug distribution services by Blue Shield --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: What is your response to what we suggested this morning on the universal practice of public liability insurance insuring not only against the risk of ultimate judgment against the policyholder but the cost of litigation, is that --

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: My response is that that example is a good illustration of our point. We think it would be absurd to contend that an agreement between an insurance company and a law firm concerning the supplying of legal services to the insurance company is the business of insurance in any ordinary sense.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: How about the provision in the policy itself?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Even if it's in the policy itself it simply begs common ordinary understanding to say that an agreement between an insurance company and a law firm about how much they're going to pay per hour is the business of insurance.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Isn't also true that medicine is part of health?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: That's true too Your Honor and our argument that will follow.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: That they can -- well, as the paying of the doctor's bill insurance?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: The paying of the claims to the insurer -- to the policyholder is the business of insurance and an agreement between the insurance company and somebody who provides services is we contend not the business of insurance.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Including the doctors?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Including the doctors, including the pharmacists, there's no difference between pharmacist and doctors.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Then under the Group Health Plan it wouldn't be covered, wouldn't the insurance would because they pay the doctors?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: I'm not certain, I understanding to your point Mr. Justice Marshall --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: They pay the doctors. The Group Health hires the doctor.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: So, that wouldn't be insurance then?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: The hiring of the doctors would not be the business of insurance.

turns.name: JUSTICE POWELL
turns.text: What if the --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: It wouldn't be -- they wouldn't be covered by this?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: It would not be part of the business of insurance and maybe perfectly lawful. There's no reason to suppose it's not lawful but it wouldn't be part of the business of insurance.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: What if forgetting any problems of legal ethics or anything, what if an insurance company just had a stable of lawyers, its employees that it furnished under its insurance contracts to beneficiaries who were sued for liability, would those employment contracts with the lawyers pay the business of insurance?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: I think not Mr. Justice Stewart. It's hard to see how those contracts had violated the antitrust laws but the relationship between although the question is not so clear. I think that's an area of --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: If it's not clear, then why is it so clear that if the insurance company does the same thing with independent contracts is it all of a sudden becomes very clear?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Well, it seems to me basis for distinction between one's dealings with ones own employees and ones dealing with outside contract.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: In each case, the insurance -- the insurer is furnishing the services and what difference has it whether so far as this exemption goes whether it does so through its employees or through independent contract?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Well, I think as I say my first response would be that I don't think labor relation agreements between an insurance company and doctors --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But you don't make the labor relations agreements, they're employment contract, no point in giving out a fancy name.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Between insurance company and its doctors, my first reaction was I don't think that will be the business of insurance although I think the question there would be closer because I think there's a basis for distinction. But the point we wish to emphasize is that at some point you have to draw the line. Relations -- insurance companies have myriad relationships with third parties what you would call independent contractors to furnish the insurance company with good and services. They contract with printers, they contract with lawyers, they contract with building owners. Congress, there's no suggestion in the legislative history the Act of Congress intended and all of those kinds of relationships to be the business of insurance.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Now, you don't seriously contend that the promise to furnish to defend that person in a case of an accident is not insurance.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: No, the promise is insurance to deliver in the pharmacy --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Alright then, I wouldn't think you would think -- if there's been an accident the companies called upon to furnish the legal services that were promised. You wouldn't think furnishing the legal service was not insurance?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: No, I agree that that would be insurance --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And if they're going to furnish to legal service they're going to have to pay for them?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: That is correct but only in the same sense.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You wouldn't if the insurance company goes out to an independent law firm and hires them to furnish the services that were promised; the furnishing of those services is part of insurance business?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Well, we can say no because --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: You just said so.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: The furnishing of the services to the policyholders, that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And but you wouldn't think that the negotiation the price at which that they were furnished was part of the insurance?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Negotiation between the insurance company and the lawyer we would contend does not the business of insurance because there is no principle basis for distinguishing that kind of agreement between insurance company --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Why not draw the line and say well, perhaps that piecemeal negotiation is one thing but a forward contract, they're sort of a supply contract in the indefinite future is something different. You don't think that one.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Because the any kind of any distinction on that basis would break down. For instance, --

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Any distinction breaks down and what about the contract of purchase the paper on which the policy is written, is that part of the business of insurance?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: The contract that purchased the paper?

turns.name: JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: On which the policy is printed.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: We would contend it would not be and that a conspiracy between printers to fix the price of printing services which used an agreement within an insurance company to do too to implement that conspiracy is not what Congress intended to exempt from the antitrust laws.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: Well, the insurance company could be a plaintiff in an antitrust lawsuit against the paper manufacturers who agreed to fix the prices.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Exactly, we contend it would be.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: It could be a plaintiff here. It could be a plaintiff here against at any pharmacist to agree among themselves to fix the prices but it isn't the question here, the question is whether --

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Under our contention -- but not on the petitioner's contention.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACKMUN
turns.text: No, no I think --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: He responded to that this morning.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: But not consistently.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But the point is that now we have an agreement with them between the insurance company and the pharmacist to provide services and the question is whether or not that is the business of insurance.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: And our response is that it is not because if it were then everything in an insurance company would do to save its own costs of services would be the business of insurance.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Of course, perhaps that's the answer.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: But Mr. Kaiser --

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: But Congress did not so intend. It's clear from this Court's decisions in Congress.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: I understood Mr. Kaiser this morning when I put the question to him to say that the action of the drugs -- drug people together independent of the agreement with Blue Shield could be an antitrust violation and one of the ways that could be asserted would be for Blue Shield to sue them because they were pushing the price up; price fixing agreement.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: That's correct Mr. Chief Justice but the distinction is important. He makes that concession and that's perfectly true. We agree with that but what his position necessarily entails is that if that conspiracy between the pharmacist were embodied in an agreement with an insurance company that would be the business of -- that agreement would make it all part of the business of insurance.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: It doesn't know his response to that was that would not render them. It might be part of the business of insurance as between Blue Shield and the druggist but it wouldn't render the druggist immune from an antitrust violation for their own agreement.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: Well, I don't understand that to be their position Your Honor. I didn't hear him to say that this morning.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: I understood that very clearly that it was his position.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: But if that's his position what he saying is that then we can violate the antitrust laws by entering into an agreement with pharmacist --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, that's sort of exemption from the antitrust laws mean.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: And the pharmacist would be guilty of antitrust and we would not.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, that's exactly an exemption means that you can violate, you can engage in conduct which would be violated of the antitrust laws if you didn't have an exemption. That's exactly what his position is.

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: But what this Court has established is that the McCarran-Ferguson Act didn't intend to immunize entities that intended to immunize conduct, agreements practices --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Except that a business, the business of insurance?

turns.name: RICHARD A. ALLEN
turns.text: That's right. But it doesn't matter who's engaging in it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Allen. You have some time left Mr. Kaiser. Do you have anything further?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Thank you. Mr. Chief Justice, if there was any confusion with our discussion earlier today it would be our position that in the event pharmacist get together and conspired to enter into a pharmacy agreement and came the Blue Shield and told us, held a gun to our head so to speak and said we're not going to enter in to this agreement with you unless you pay us a certain price and that --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, what if Blue Shield joined the conspiracy?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Your Honor that if we are a victim of a conspiracy ---

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I didn't say that. I said they are party to it.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Under those -- like we have two different circumstances under my theory --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, yes I know but I'm asking about mine now. How about Blue Shield is party to the conspiracy?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Alright Your Honor, if Blue Shield was a party to the conspiracy --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Remember they are carrying out the business of insurance according to your view.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: It would be my initial impression right now that if the agreement was embodied into the pharmacy agreement and it is always been our position that the pharmacy agreement is immune as a part of the business of insurance. Well, then there would be no violation.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Blue Shield would be immune?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: How about -- and but nevertheless I suppose you say the druggist --

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: For the separate conspiracy between the druggist there maybe an actionable that maybe actionable conspiracy.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: But Blue Shield is at least an ordinary human experience isn't as likely to -- isn't very likely to enter into conspiracy to raise their own costs?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor that would be totally against our own interest and the interest of our policyholders.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: So, the hypothesis is at least economically not very real?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Unless they get together to keep Blue Shield from going too low and Blue Shield gets paid off in some way?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes, I guess that is the possibility Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, it has been true, hasn't it in some places?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Not to my knowledge Your Honor. I'm not familiar with anything of that nature that's ever happened.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Is the price fixing agreement have to be the fix maximum prices to be unlawful supposing it's an agreement to fix prices is much lower than anybody else can charge?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Your Honor, there's considerable dispute about that but as I read the cases of price fixing can either be to fix maximum prices or minimal price.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: It may be illegal either way, would it not?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Strictly from the antitrust from an act standpoint.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: So they could have a lot of group of large druggist who wanted to have a very low price in order to drive small companies out of business for example and persuaded Blue Shield to go along with it and that would normally be an antitrust violation?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Well, I'm operating my thoughts are under the theory that Blue Shield would offer this agreement to everyone; somewhat to possibly be able to accept some possibly not.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Is there any limitation now, can anyone be a participant?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Any license pharmacy in the State of Texas has been invited and is capable of becoming a participating pharmacy.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Mr. Allen to be sure that my confusion is completely cleared up, do you agree with the view that in order to qualify for the exemption under Section 2 (b) of the McCarran Act you must establish both that you're in the business of insurance and secondly that that which you claim is an exemption is regulated by state law?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. We claim that we -- it must be constitute a part of the business of insurance and the activity must be regulated by state law. And we believe that undeniably has in this case. Your Honor, we have talked considerably today about --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I take it that the court below did not reach the second step. They just simply said, it's not the business of insurance.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: The Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit did not reach the second step. However, the trial court found that it had been regulated in its words pervasively.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But the Court of Appeals didn't reach that second?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: No sir. It said that, in view of the fact that it was finding at the pharmacy agreement did not constitute business of insurance. It was not required to go to the second step relating to state regulation nor was it required to go to the step relating to boycott. The business of insurance is a federal question. There seems to have been considerable discussions today about the state's interests and whether or not the state has regulated and whether or not the state considered the matter to be the business of insurance. While we believe --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It's true that the exemption however exist only at the exemption for the business of insurance exist only to the extent that it is regulated by state law?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So, and your opponent in part 2 I think it is what the brief asserts that this is not regulated by state law. So, that's reason that became relevant as I understood that relevance of that argument.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes sir, well, I just want to make our point clear. We're not relying upon what the state did to say that this constitutes the business of insurance. We're relying strictly upon a federal standard.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: But the federal standard upon that federal standard is that the business of insurance has to be regulated by state law?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But suppose the state says that the dealing in drugs is not an insurance function and therefore we will not regulate it. Then, what would your position be?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: My position would still be the same Your Honor. The business of insurance is a federal question. If it happens to be the business of insurance but not regulated, the exemption does not attach. Although, we do believe that federal court should give certain --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, if they said it was and they did move and it would be a very question.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: I'm sorry Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: If they said it was an insurance problem and did regulate it, it would not be a federal? You don't go that way, do you?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: I think its still would be a federal consideration, Your Honor. The business of insurance is a federal question to be decided by the federal courts. We do not suggest that everything in insurance company does constitutes the business of insurance. An insurance company may do many things in the conduct of its business which are not directly related to the furnishing of specific benefits and which are not related directly to the cost of those specific benefits. What we are saying in our position here today is that the pharmacy agreement directly violates --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, what would some of those things be -- let's assume a company that's an insurance company nothing else --

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: It's not a conglomerate or anything. So, only in the insurance business now, what would all -- what are those many things be --

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: As the Solicitor General has said that the contracts for the purchase of paper (Voice Overlap) the buildings --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, how can that be the building of insurance?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: That I would say it would not --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: If it's in the insurance business then it has to buy paper.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Your Honor, the federal courts and this Court has said that the key is not whether or not it's in the insurance business but the business of insurance and there's a difference --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Well, it's in the business of insurance and then conducting at business it has to have policies?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Whether written or printed on paper and why therefore, shouldn't its purchase of paper be the business of insurance?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Because there is not a requisite relationship between the insurer and its policyholder in that situation.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, about the paper used for -- that that they send out to the policyholder to submit claims on?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: That still would not really involve the claim -- the risk --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Condition of the policy is that you file a claim on this form?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Your Honor, I think that would be too attenuated to the business of insurance to become an exempt to activity.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: What about the between the insurance company and the lawyer to employees to defend the casualty case?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Now, we're back where we started.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: I want to go back where we started on the lawyer hired by the insurance company to defend the policyholder who sued under a liability policy.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Would that be the business of insurance?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: If it was an obligation contained in the policy of insurance?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: The policy says, “We'll defend all claims.”

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: And if it was --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: It doesn't say by lawyers or how but then they go and hire lawyers to defend the claims.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: If it is a part of the coverage and benefits provided by the policy and if it is something that is eminently related to the insured --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, I don't think I don't need any adjectives. Yes or no, is it covered in your view or not? Is it part of the business of insurance?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: I think it might be Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: You think it might be?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: You're not very confident and yet I don't see any difference between that in providing drug as I indicated earlier. I don't understand your lack of confidence on that case in your complete confidence on this case.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Your Honor, it's the difference between that. The conceptual difference between the two types of cases here, I agree with Your Honor's hypothesis. I think I agree and not I think I agree but I do agree. However, we're looking at a different type of insurance and a different type of policy here. We're looking at one where this particular --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Supposing you pay money and you go out and borrow money from a bank to pay off a claim in dollars, is that the business of insurance?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: If the insured?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: No, the company has a little short of cash so they go to the bank to raise the cash to pay off a very large claim at some catastrophe of some kind.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't believe that would constitute --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Well, how's that different?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Well, it does -- I believe that it would fall too far outside the relationship between the insurer and the insured. The assumption of risk in providing a benefit --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: What the insurance company needs to satisfy a claim that it's obligated and under the policy to satisfy, it's $10.00 rather than drugs. I don't see the difference.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: I don't believe it would fall within the criteria establish by this Court in SEC versus National Securities.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: The only criteria there is that it be the business of insurance. It's kind of a question begging criteria --

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, this Court set out --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: And if you buy the company.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Set out some very broad criteria in determining what constitutes the business of insurance in that case but the real nexus for what does constitute the business of insurance are matters that intimately relate to the insurer-insured relationship. The Court also said that the type of policy which could be issued its reliability, interpretation and enforcement and other activities of insurers which relate closely to their status as reliable insurers constitute the business of insurance for McCarran purposes.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Do you apply that all that language to make a big loan from a bank to pay-off a policy obligation it seems to me?

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Your Honor, there was the preceding case in 1959 of Securities and Exchange Commissioner versus Variable Annuity said, that the merger of two insurance companies even though the sale of stock or the holding of stock involved the insureds to some respect said that that was not sufficient that did not sufficiently relate to the insurer-insured relationship. And I think your proposition of that going out and borrowing money would be more akin to the Variable Annuity case.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: The real answer is, to my brother Stevens' question is that the further we take you away from this case and your theory of the less certain you get.

turns.name: KEITH E. KAISER
turns.text: Yes sir. Thank you very much Your Honors.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Thank you Mr. Kaiser. Thank you gentlemen. The case is submitted.
